Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit chaotic, but I made it work. I'd usually block out time in the evenings after my part-time work and other responsibilities, spreading my 22 hours across the week as best I could. I'm a visual learner, so I'd create colorful mind maps and diagrams to organize concepts. Some weeks were more structured than others depending on what was due, but I always tried to keep moving forward even when things felt overwhelming.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and what else was happening in my life. With limited resources at home, I sometimes had to study at the library or wait until I had reliable internet access. When assignments were due, I'd cram in more hours, but during quieter weeks, I might spread things out more. The inconsistency stressed me out, but I learned to adapt and make the most of whatever time I had.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new assignments or seeing if grades had been posted that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get anxious about missing something important, so I'd log in just to make sure I hadn't overlooked a deadline. When I was feeling really overwhelmed, I'd avoid logging in for a bit because seeing all the tasks would spike my anxiety. But eventually the fear of falling behind would push me back.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a natural habit for me—it always felt like something I had to force myself to do. I'd set reminders on my phone to check it, especially around assignment deadlines. After a few weeks, checking once or twice a day became more automatic, but it never felt comfortable. The anxiety around it made it hard to just casually browse the way some students might.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly helpful for me since I learn best visually. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. The discussion forums were also useful when I needed clarification on confusing topics—reading other students' questions helped me realize I wasn't alone in struggling. Any resource with visual elements or step-by-step explanations felt most valuable to my learning style.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading PDFs because they were text-heavy and hard for me to process without visual aids. Some of the optional supplementary materials got ignored because I simply didn't have time with everything else I was juggling. The discussion posts I made were minimal because participating felt draining when I was already anxious about keeping up with required work. I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a clear connection to my assignments or exams. If it had visual components—like diagrams, videos, or interactive elements—I was much more likely to engage deeply. I also valued activities where I could see immediate progress or get feedback. With limited time and resources, I couldn't afford to spend energy on things that felt disconnected from my success.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required activities came with the pressure of grades, so they always took priority even when I was exhausted or anxious. Optional ones sometimes made me feel guilty—like I should be doing them but couldn't manage it. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually because I was genuinely curious, which made it feel more enjoyable but also more stressful knowing I was 'wasting' time.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study them later at home where my connection was unreliable. I filled notebooks with hand-drawn diagrams and color-coded notes. I also used free YouTube tutorials and borrowed textbooks from the library to supplement what was on the LMS. Most of my actual learning happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours redrawing diagrams from the lectures, testing myself with flashcards I'd made, or working through problems in my notebook. None of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also study with a small group of classmates in person, quizzing each other and explaining concepts. The LMS would show I'd watched a video once, but it couldn't capture the five times I reviewed my notes on it afterward.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a lecture video or PDF once, download it, and then work with it offline for days or even weeks. My internet access isn't always reliable, so I learned early on to grab everything I needed in one session. The LMS probably made it look like I only engaged briefly, but those downloaded materials were my lifeline throughout the course.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and engaging felt nearly impossible. When multiple deadlines piled up or when I was dealing with stress at home, I'd feel paralyzed. The sadness would creep in and make everything feel pointless. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. The CBT sessions helped me push through eventually, but those low points were really difficult.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by competing responsibilities, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—just enough to not fall behind. Confusion about course material would make me avoid certain sections until I absolutely had to face them. Fatigue made it hard to concentrate during video lectures, so I'd have to rewatch them later when I had more energy, which the system wouldn't capture.
Q14: There were times I'd have a lecture video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about finances or family stuff. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything because I was too anxious to focus. Sometimes I'd open materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, even though I knew I wasn't really learning. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through the motions.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts and some readings because they felt less urgent than assignments. When my anxiety was high, I'd avoid anything that felt uncertain or confusing—I'd tell myself I'd tackle it when I felt better. Sometimes I'd procrastinate on starting assignments because the fear of not doing well enough was paralyzing. I knew it was counterproductive, but the avoidance felt like self-protection in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd become hyper-focused in the days leading up to a due date, logging in multiple times to check requirements and submit work. Without a deadline looming, my engagement would drop significantly because I'd focus on more immediate life pressures. The deadline-driven approach was stressful but effective—it's how I maintained my high assignment completion rate despite everything.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on me emotionally and motivationally. Positive feedback gave me a boost that would carry me through the next few tasks. Critical feedback, even when constructive, would sometimes send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd question everything. After getting feedback, I'd often go back and review related materials more carefully. It definitely shaped how I approached subsequent assignments.
Q18: The course structure with clear deadlines and organized modules helped me plan around my chaotic schedule. Having videos and materials released in a predictable pattern meant I could plan my downloads and study sessions. However, when too many things were due at once, the structure felt suffocating rather than helpful. I needed flexibility that the rigid structure didn't always allow for someone in my situation.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement much easier for my learning style. Having materials available for download was crucial given my internet situation. What made it harder was when there were long text-heavy readings with no visual alternatives, or when participation requirements felt demanding given everything else I was managing. More flexibility in how to demonstrate engagement would have helped reduce my anxiety.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways—logging in frequently out of anxiety or to check on things, watching videos multiple times to understand, but then struggling to actually complete work due to other barriers. Maybe they're engaged mentally but facing obstacles like time constraints, confusion, or personal issues that prevent them from submitting. High activity doesn't always mean everything is going smoothly.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. My attendance was low and my LMS engagement metrics probably looked concerning, but I was doing a lot of work offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. I earned a Distinction because I understood the material deeply through my own study methods, not because I was constantly visible on the platform. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual effort.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and submitted work—the basic timeline of activity. But they completely miss the quality and depth of offline engagement, the emotional state while studying, and whether someone is truly learning or just clicking through. They can't capture the hours I spent with printed notes, the stress I was managing, or the resilience it took to keep going despite everything.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and putting in real mental effort, not just completing tasks mechanically. For me, it's about connecting with concepts visually, asking questions when confused, and pushing through even when anxiety makes me want to give up. It's showing up mentally and emotionally, even when I can't always show up physically or digitally. Engagement is about commitment to learning despite obstacles.
Q24: The LMS data would never show how much anxiety I battled just to open the platform, or how the CBT sessions helped me develop coping strategies to keep going. It wouldn't capture the tears of frustration or the small victories when concepts finally clicked during offline study. It can't show how I balanced coursework with limited resources and family responsibilities, or how much resilience it took to earn that Distinction. My journey was so much more than login counts and timestamps.
